Article.ArticleTitle: Implications of asymptomatic hypotension for patients prescribed sacubitril/valsartan: Insight from the REVIEW-HF registry.

Abstract.AbstractText: Sacubitril/valsartan has unequivocally demonstrated prognostic improvement in patients with chronic heart failure in large-scale randomized controlled trials. However, it remains uncertain whether patients presenting with asymptomatic hypotension have distinct prognoses compared to those with symptomatic hypotension, and there is no consensus on the optimal strategy for managing asymptomatic hypotension.

A multicenter retrospective study was conducted, analyzing data from patients initiated on sacubitril/valsartan therapy. The primary outcome was a composite of cardiovascular death or a hospitalization for heart failure. Among 992 patients, 72 patients (7.3 %) experienced symptomatic hypotension, and 122 patients (12.3 %) experienced asymptomatic hypotension within three months. The incidence of primary endpoint for patients who experienced hypotension, regardless of whether it was symptomatic or asymptomatic, was higher than for those who did not experience hypotension (Log-rank p < 0.05). Among patients who experienced asymptomatic hypotension, 23 patients discontinued sacubitril/valsartan within three days after the onset of asymptomatic hypotension. Patients who discontinued sacubitril/valsartan after the occurrence of asymptomatic hypotension experienced a significantly higher incidence of primary endpoints compared to those who continued sacubitril/valsartan therapy (Log-rank p = 0.01).

The prognosis of patients who discontinued sacubitril/valsartan after the occurrence of asymptomatic hypotension during sacubitril/valsartan therapy was poor. It may be beneficial to aim for the continuation of sacubitril/valsartan therapy even after the occurrence of asymptomatic hypotension.

Chemical.NameOfSubstance: sacubitril and valsartan sodium hydrate drug combination

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declaration of competing interest S.I. received remuneration from Otsuka Pharmaceutical Co. and Novartis Pharma. T.S has received honoraria from Novartis Pharmaceuticals KK and Otsuka Pharmaceuticals Co. Ltd. H.T. received remuneration from AstraZeneca plc, Ono Pharmaceutical Company, Limited. Pfizer Inc., Otsuka Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Daiichi Sankyo Company, Limited, Eli Lilly and Company, Boehringer Ingelheim GmbH, Abbott Japan LLC, and Novartis International AG. N.K. was affiliated with a department funded by Paramount Bed and received research grants from AstraZeneca, AMI Inc., and EchoNous Inc., and honoraria from Otsuka Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Eli Lilly, Nippon Boehringer Ingelheim, and Novartis Pharma. K. Kida received honoraria from AstraZeneca K.K., Ono Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Nippon Boehringer Ingelheim Co., Ltd., Bayer Yakuhin Ltd., Otsuka Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., and Novartis Pharmaceuticals Co., Ltd.
